region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for wafer biscuit as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for packaged snack biscuits is limited.

allergens: may contain nuts depending on flavour

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of waffle biscuit is approximately 30 g, equivalent to 3–4 individual wafer fingers or one small packet.

nutrient_source.title: Wafer biscuit, cream-filled — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for waffle biscuit (any flavour), CR ID F11080079. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Wafer biscuits, cream-filled — GI value estimated from analogous cream-filled wafer biscuit entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 (high) is based on analogous cream-filled wafer biscuit entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 66–76 for similar refined-flour, cream-filled wafer products. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (70 × 19.998 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = approximately 14.0. This is consistent with the input record's embedded GI value of 70.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (33.3 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 214 mg/100 g adds to overall dietary sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid waffle biscuits: the combination of 33.3 g/100 g sugar, zero dietary fibre, and 214 mg/100 g sodium promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 33.3 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 66.7 g/100 g with GI 70 and zero dietary fibre produces a very high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Waffle biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: sugar at 33.3 g/100 g and a high GI of 70 with zero fibre result in rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation and should be avoided entirely in a diabetes management diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~10.7 g/100 g from palm-based vegetable fat, high sugar 33.3 g/100 g, and ultra-processed formulation with zero fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Waffle biscuits pose a high cardiovascular risk due to elevated saturated fat (~10.7 g/100 g from palm oil), high refined sugar (33.3 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre; regular consumption is associated with raised LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular disease risk per WHO and AHA guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Waffle biscuit (any flavour) is a crispy, layered wafer-style biscuit made from thin sheets of baked wheat flour dough sandwiched with a sweetened flavoured cream filling. Common flavours include vanilla, chocolate, strawberry, and pandan. The texture is characteristically light, crunchy, and airy, with a sweet, mildly buttery filling. It is an ultra-processed snack food widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in individual fingers or multi-pack formats. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 476 kcal/100 g, with high total fat (21.4 g/100 g, predominantly from palm oil), very high sugar (33.3 g/100 g), and high refined carbohydrate (66.7 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), providing no glycaemic buffering. Sodium is 214 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is high (GI 70), making it unsuitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, this food is contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and sodium content combined with zero fibre. It is best treated as an occasional treat and avoided in therapeutic dietary contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a wafer biscuit?
Is waffle biscuit high in sugar?
Can I eat wafer biscuits if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of wafer biscuits?
Are cream wafer biscuits suitable for heart patients?
威化饼干适合糖尿病人吗?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080079) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 70 is consistent with analogous cream-filled wafer biscuit entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 50% of total fat based on typical palm-oil-based wafer biscuit formulation, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for similar products.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 10.71 g/100 g (approximately 50% of total fat of 21.42 g/100 g), based on typical palm-oil-dominant fat profile for cream-filled wafer biscuits; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central entries for similar products (e.g., USDA SR Legacy item 18133 — wafers, vanilla). Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin and mineral micronutrients (except calcium and iron), and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and no supplementary approved-source data was available to populate these fields. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 g/100 g per the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the source record. Dietary fibre is confirmed 0.0 g/100 g per HPB source — this is consistent with the refined wheat flour and cream filling formulation of this ultra-processed product. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cream-filled wafer biscuits; the input record's embedded GI value of 70 is consistent with this estimate. Glycaemic load per 30 g serving = (70 × 19.998) / 100 ≈ 14.0.
